input.incident: I missed a meeting with my manager.

giftIdeas.title: Same-day flowers or local florist bouquet
giftIdeas.whyItFits: Neutral and widely appropriate; search locally rather than guessing inventory.

giftIdeas.title: Premium chocolate or snack gift box
giftIdeas.whyItFits: Coworker-safe when interests are unknown; easy to verify via search.

giftIdeas.title: Small desk plant or succulent
giftIdeas.whyItFits: Low-risk tone; avoids strong personal taste assumptions.

plan: Acknowledge what went wrong without deflecting.
Offer one specific repair action (time, place, or gesture).
Ask what they need to feel respected.
Follow up once after a pause—no pressure.
risks: They may need time before responding.
Long or repeated messages can feel like pressure.

writing.subject: Re: missed meeting
writing.apologyMessage: I apologize for missing our meeting. I mismanaged my schedule and didn't prioritize our time together. I value our discussions and would like to reschedule at your earliest convenience. Please let me know what works for you.
writing.followUpMessage: I wanted to check in and see if you had a chance to think about rescheduling our meeting. I'm here whenever you're ready.
writing.optionalLightFraming: I understand this may have caused some inconvenience.

email.subject: Re: missed meeting
email.body:
I apologize for missing our meeting. I mismanaged my schedule and didn't prioritize our time together. I value our discussions and would like to reschedule at your earliest convenience. Please let me know what works for you.

I understand this may have caused some inconvenience.

—

I wanted to check in and see if you had a chance to think about rescheduling our meeting. I'm here whenever you're ready.

followup.title: Follow up: missed meeting
followup.notes:
• No excuses; center impact on them.
• Keep it short.
• Include one concrete repair action.
• Do not invent facts or detailed alibis.

Plan: Acknowledge what went wrong without deflecting. Offer one specific repair action (time, place, or gesture). Ask what they need to feel respected. Follow up once after a pause—no pressure.
